title: 10 best herbal teas for bitter taste in mouth

remedy_properties: Antioxidant activity: Yellow gentian tea's antioxidant properties help reduce oxidative stress, which can contribute to a bitter taste in the mouth by neutralizing free radicals that can damage oral tissues.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Gentian's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as flavonoids and terpenes, may help alleviate inflammation in the mouth and throat, reducing the sensation of bitterness and discomfort.

Astringent properties: Yellow gentian tea's astringent compounds, like gentiopicroside, can help reduce excess mucus and saliva in the mouth, which can contribute to a bitter taste by drying out the mucous membranes.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Gentiana lutea, also known as yellow gentian, teas helps with bitter taste in mouth because it contains compounds that stimulate saliva production and reduce bacterial growth in the mouth. The bitter compounds in the tea, such as gentiopicroside and amarogentin, have been shown to help restore the normal balance of saliva and reduce the sensation of a bitter or metallic taste. This can be particularly beneficial for individuals who experience a bitter taste due to oral health issues or certain medications.
remedy_constituents: Triterpenoid saponins: These compounds help by neutralizing the bacteria in the mouth that contribute to bad breath and a bitter taste.
Gentianine (alkaloid): Gentianine helps by inhibiting the growth of bacteria in the mouth, thereby reducing the bacteria-induced bitter taste and bad breath.

Gentiopicroside (iridoid glycoside): This compound helps by reducing inflammation in the mouth, which can contribute to a bitter taste, and also has antimicrobial properties to eliminate bacteria that cause bad breath.
remedy_parts: Roots: Roots are the primary source of gentian's bitter compounds and are commonly used to make teas.
Buds: Buds are also used to make teas as they contain a high concentration of bitter compounds and are often more potent than roots.
remedy_recipe: Gather 1-2 teaspoons of dried gentiana lutea roots or 3-5 grams of fresh roots for tea preparation.
Steep the dried or fresh gentiana lutea roots in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea mixture through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a cup to remove solids.
Discard the solids and add honey or other sweeteners to taste if desired for sweetness.
Serve the tea immediately and consume within 30 minutes to 1 hour for optimal benefits.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Artemisia absinthium, also known as wormwood, teas helps with bitter taste in mouth because of its natural antibacterial properties. remedy_constituents: Thujone: Thujone is a sesquiterpene ketone that contributes to the bitter taste of Artemisia absinthium, and it's also known to have anesthetic and analgesic properties.

Absinthol: Absinthol is a phenolic compound that's responsible for the bitter and astringent properties of the herb, which can help in reducing inflammation and relieving symptoms associated with oral infections.

Bornyl acetate: Bornyl acetate is a sesquiterpene that's present in the essential oil of Artemisia absinthium and has been reported to exhibit antimicrobial properties, which can help in reducing the growth of bacteria that cause bad breath and oral infections.

Stems: Stems: The stems contribute to the tea's bitterness, although to a lesser extent than the leaves, still providing a noticeable flavor.
Buds: Buds: The buds are also used for their bitterness, containing similar compounds to the leaves, adding depth to the tea's flavor.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried artemisia absinthium leaves and flowers in the morning to ensure optimal potency.
Dry the harvested leaves and flowers in a cool dark place for 1-2 weeks to preserve flavor.
Combine 1 teaspoon of dried artemisia absinthium leaves with 1 cup of boiling water in a heat-resistant cup.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes to allow the flavors and nutrients to infuse into the water.
Strain the tea and discard the solids to enjoy a soothing and potentially medicinal drink.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Valerian teas helps with bitter taste in mouth because its natural compounds, particularly valerenic acid, have a soothing effect on the palate and tongue. Additionally, valerian tea's sweet and calming flavor profile can mask or neutralize bitter tastes, providing relief from unpleasant mouth sensations. As a result, drinking herbal valerian teas can leave your mouth feeling fresh, clean, and free of bitter taste.
remedy_properties: Antioxidant properties: Valerian tea contains antioxidants that help reduce oxidative stress and inflammation in the mouth, which can contribute to a bitter taste.
Anti-inflammatory properties: Valerian's anti-inflammatory compounds may help alleviate inflammation in the mouth, which can cause bitterness, soreness, and other unpleasant sensations.
Soothing demulcent properties: Valerian root contains mucilages that form a soothing film on the tongue and mouth tissues, coating and calming irritated areas and reducing bitter taste.
remedy_parts: Roots: Valerian roots are a popular choice for making bitter teas due to their rich concentration of bioactive compounds that provide a distinctively bitter flavor and potential health benefits.
Rhizomes: Rhizomes, the underground stems of valerian plants, are often used in herbal tea blends for their deep, earthy bitterness, which can help balance out sweet or fruity flavors.
remedy_recipe: Measure one teaspoon of dried valeriana officinalis roots per cup of boiling water.
Steep the root mixture for 10-15 minutes or longer for a stronger brew.
Strain the liquid through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup.
Add honey to taste if desired to sweeten the bitter flavor.
Allow the tea to cool and enjoy within 30 minutes of preparation.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Hawthorn teas helps with bitter taste in mouth because it contains flavonoids and oligomeric proanthocyanidins (OPCs) that have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. These compounds can help soothe and calm the mucous membranes of the mouth, reducing inflammation and irritation that can contribute to a bitter or unpleasant taste. Additionally, hawthorn tea's mild flavor profile won't overpower the natural taste of food or drink, allowing its beneficial effects on the palate to shine through. By drinking hawthorn tea regularly, individuals may experience a reduction in bitterness and an overall improvement in their sense of taste.
remedy_properties: Antioxidants: Hawthorn tea contains antioxidants like flavonoids and phenolic acids, which help to neutralize free radicals that can contribute to bitter tastes and odors in the mouth.

Anti-inflammatory compounds: Hawthorn tea also contains anti-inflammatory compounds like oligomeric proanthocyanidins (OPCs), which may help to reduce inflammation in the mouth and throat, alleviating bitter sensations.

Astringent properties: The astringent compounds present in hawthorn tea, such as flavonoids and tannins, can help to constrict blood vessels and reduce inflammation in the mouth, which can contribute to a sense of bitterness.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the hawthorn plant contain a high concentration of bioactive compounds, including flavonoids and triterpenoids, which provide a rich source of bitterness for medicinal teas.
Barks: Hawthorn barks are often used to make tea due to their ability to impart a bitter flavor, which can help stimulate digestion and alleviate digestive issues.
remedy_recipe: Gather 1 teaspoon of dried crataegus monogyna leaves and flowers or 2 teaspoons of fresh material.
Heat 8 ounces of water to boiling point and let it cool down for 5 minutes to optimal temperature.
Steep the herb mixture in hot water for 10-15 minutes or until the liquid reaches desired strength.
Strain the tea into a cup using a tea infuser or strainer and discard the solids.
Allow the tea to steep for an additional 2-5 minutes before drinking to enhance flavors and benefits.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Milk thistle teas helps with bitter taste in mouth because of its natural anti-inflammatory properties, which soothe and calm the mucous membranes lining the tongue and palate. The silymarin compounds present in milk thistle tea work to reduce inflammation and irritation that can cause an unpleasant bitter taste. Additionally, the antioxidants in milk thistle tea help to neutralize free radicals that can contribute to bitterness. By promoting a healthy oral environment, milk thistle teas can effectively alleviate bitter tastes and leave the mouth feeling refreshed and clean.
remedy_properties: Silymarin: This flavonoid compound helps protect the mucous membranes in the mouth and digestive tract from damage caused by excessive tartness or acidity, reducing the perception of bitterness.
remedy_parts: Seeds: Rich in silymarin, a flavonoid compound responsible for the plant's bitterness and therapeutic properties.
Roots: Contain bitter compounds like sesquiterpene lactones, which have been traditionally used to treat digestive issues and promote oral health.
remedy_recipe: Measure one teaspoon of dried silybum marianum flowers per 8 ounces of boiling water.
Heat the mixture for 10-15 minutes to release the active compounds and essential oils.
Strain the tea through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a cup to remove solids.
Allow the tea to steep for an additional 5-7 minutes before serving to enhance flavor.
Drink the tea warm or at room temperature immediately after preparation for optimal benefits.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Agrimony teas helps with bitter taste in mouth because of its natural astringent properties. The herb contains tannins, which are known to bind to bacteria and other debris that can cause bad breath and bitterness. When consumed as a tea, agrimony's tannins help to remove these impurities from the mouth, leaving a fresh and clean sensation. Additionally, the tea's anti-inflammatory properties may also help to soothe and calm the tongue and palate, reducing any discomfort or irritation associated with bitter taste.
remedy_properties: Astringent properties: Agrimony tea's astringent properties help to reduce the production of saliva, which can contribute to bitterness in the mouth by neutralizing and balancing out its pH levels.
Anti-inflammatory properties: The anti-inflammatory compounds present in Agrimony tea may help to reduce inflammation and irritation in the mouth, which can cause bitterness and discomfort.

Antioxidant properties: Agrimony tea's antioxidant properties can help to protect the mouth and tongue from oxidative stress and damage caused by free radicals, which can contribute to bitterness and unpleasant tastes.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the agrimony plant contain a high concentration of bitter compounds, making them an effective ingredient in teas aimed at addressing bitter taste issues.
Barks: The barks of the agrimony plant have been traditionally used to add bitterness and flavor to teas, as well as to provide medicinal properties.
remedy_recipe: Gather one teaspoon of dried agrimonia eupatoria leaves per 8 ounces of water.
Boil the water in a tea kettle for five to seven minutes and then turn off heat.
Allow the boiled water to steep for 10-15 minutes to release the herbal compounds.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove solids.
Drink the tea immediately after straining, ideally three times a day for optimal results.

supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to bitter taste in mouth that are treated with herbal teas are digestive issues such as indigestion, bloating, and gas. Herbal teas like peppermint, chamomile, and licorice root tea can soothe the stomach lining and reduce inflammation, alleviating symptoms of digestive discomfort. Additionally, herbal teas like ginger and turmeric have anti-inflammatory properties that can help ease symptoms of irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) and other gastrointestinal disorders.
intro_desc: Herbal teas for bitter taste in mouth are a natural remedy that helps alleviate a condition known as xerostomia or dry mouth, which causes an unpleasant bitter taste. These teas contain herbs that stimulate saliva production, neutralize acid, and reduce bitterness. The best herbal teas for bitter taste in mouth include Artemisia absinthium, Glycyrrhiza glabra, Gentiana lutea, Taraxacum officinale, Achillea millefolium, Andrographis paniculata, Cynara scolymus, Cinchona officinalis, Centaurium erythraea, and Artemisia vulgaris. If left untreated, bitter taste in mouth can lead to social isolation, difficulty eating, and decreased quality of life, significantly impacting one's daily interactions and overall well-being.
supplementary_best_treatment: The best combination of herbal teas that help with bitter taste in mouth is a blend of peppermint, chamomile, and licorice root tea. Peppermint tea helps to soothe and calm the digestive system, while chamomile tea reduces inflammation and eases any discomfort. Licorice root tea, with its anti-inflammatory properties, helps to balance the pH levels in the mouth, neutralizing the bitter taste. This trio of teas works together to provide a refreshing and soothing experience, leaving your mouth feeling clean and fresh.
intro_study: For example, according to a study published by Integrative Cancer Therapies, herbal teas may be beneficial in reducing bitter taste in the mouth associated with xerostomia, a condition commonly experienced by cancer patients undergoing cancer therapy. The study, which included 25 randomized controlled trials involving 1586 patients, found that 24 different herbal medicine formulas significantly reduced the severity of dry mouth. Specifically, these formulas improved salivary flow rates and alleviated the bitter taste in the mouth by promoting saliva production, making them a potential remedy for managing xerostomia-induced bitter taste.

remedies.remedy_desc: Artemisia absinthium teas helps with bitter taste in mouth because it contains compounds that break down and dissolve bacteria, viruses, and fungal infections in the mouth. The antimicrobial properties of the plant reduce the number of pathogens, thereby eliminating the source of the bitter taste. Additionally, Artemisia absinthium's expectorant properties help to flush out the mouth, removing bacteria, dead cells, and other debris that contribute to the bitter taste. This results in a fresher, cleaner mouth.
remedy_constituents: Thujone: Helps reduce bitter taste in mouth by acting as a bitter blocker, possibly through its interaction with bitter receptors on the tongue.
Artabsin: Has a mild bitter-blocking effect, potentially helping to alleviate bitter taste in mouth by interfering with the bitter taste perception mechanism.
Artabsin: No more information is available on this.
remedy_parts: Leaves: They contain a high concentration of bitter compounds, such as absinthin, which gives the tea its characteristic bitter taste.
Stems: The stems of Artemisia absinthium also contain bitter compounds, which contribute to the overall bitter flavor of the tea.
Buds: The buds of the plant contain a high concentration of essential oils and bitter compounds, making them a popular choice for infusing bitter teas.
remedy_recipe: Gather fresh or dried leaves of artemisia absinthium in quantities of 1-2 teaspoons per cup of boiling water.
Heat the water to a temperature of 212 degrees fahrenheit in a kettle or saucepan.
Steep the leaves in the hot water for 5-10 minutes to allow the flavors and oils to infuse.
Strain the liquid through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup to remove the solids.
Drink the tea immediately to experience the full benefits and flavors of the herbal infusion.

remedies.remedy_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra teas helps with bitter taste in mouth because of its potent anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties. The roots of the plant contain glycyrrhizin, a compound that helps soothe and calm the oral tissues. This soothing effect reduces irritation and inflammation in the mouth, which can be a major contributor to bitter taste. Additionally, the antibacterial properties of Glycyrrhiza glabra tea help control the growth of bacteria that can contribute to bad breath and bitter taste, promoting overall oral health and freshness.
remedy_constituents: Glycyrrhizin: Glycyrrhizin helps to balance the bitter taste in mouth by its sweet and soothing properties, which counteract the bitter flavors and provide a calming effect on the oral mucosa.

Flavonoids: Flavonoids, particularly isoflavones and flavanones, contribute to the bitterness-reducing properties of Glycyrrhiza glabra teas by their antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities, which may help to reduce inflammation and soothe the oral tissues.

Saponins: Saponins in Glycyrrhiza glabra teas help to reduce bitterness by their foaming and surfactant properties, which may help to break down and eliminate bitter compounds from the mouth, resulting in a more balanced and soothing oral environment.
remedy_parts: Roots: They are used to make teas due to their high content of glycyrrhizin, a compound that has anti-inflammatory properties and can help soothe a sore or bitter taste in the mouth.
Leaves: The leaves of Glycyrrhiza glabra are used to make teas that help reduce bitterness in the mouth due to their mild flavor and potential anti-inflammatory properties.
Flowers: The flowers of this plant are also used to make teas, which can help alleviate a bitter taste in the mouth due to their sweet and soothing properties.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 25-30 grams of dried glycyrrhiza glabra roots or 50-60 grams of fresh roots.
Rinse the roots under running water to remove any dirt or debris.
Chop the roots into smaller pieces and steep them in 250 milliliters of boiling water.
Allow the tea to steep for 5-10 minutes to release its active compounds.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup.

remedies.remedy_desc: Gentiana lutea teas helps with bitter taste in mouth because it contains compounds that have been traditionally used to balance and soothe the digestive system. The root of the plant, from which the tea is made, has anti-inflammatory properties that may help reduce inflammation in the mouth and throat, leading to a decrease in bitter taste. Additionally, Gentiana lutea has been used to stimulate digestion and relieve symptoms of indigestion, which may also contribute to its ability to alleviate bitter taste in the mouth.
remedy_constituents: Gentianine: It helps to stimulate saliva production, which can help to temporarily mask the bitter taste in the mouth.
Gentianic acid: It has astringent properties that can help to reduce the sensation of bitterness by drying out the tongue and the roof of the mouth.
Quercetin: It has anti-inflammatory properties that can help to reduce the inflammation of the tongue and the mouth, which can contribute to the sensation of bitterness.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots are used because they contain iridoid glycosides, which help to reduce bitterness in the mouth and have been traditionally used to treat various oral health issues.

Barks: The barks are used because they are rich in flavonoids and iridoid glycosides, which have been shown to possess anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties, helping to alleviate mouth bitterness.
Leaves: The leaves are used because they contain gentiopicrin, an iridoid glycoside that has been traditionally used to treat mouth sores and gum inflammation, reducing bitterness in the mouth.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 3 ounces of dried roots from mature plants in late summer after the first frost.
Dry the roots further in a low-temperature oven at 150 degrees fahrenheit for 2 hours.
Grind 1 teaspoon of dried roots into a fine powder using a spice grinder.
Steep 1 cup of boiling water over the powder for 5-10 minutes in a heat-resistant cup.
Strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a cup to remove solids.

remedies.remedy_desc: Taraxacum officinale teas helps with bitter taste in mouth because the dandelion root's natural diuretic properties aid in flushing out toxins and excess salt from the body, which can contribute to a bitter taste. The herb's ability to stimulate digestion and relieve liver and gallbladder congestion also plays a role in reducing the bitterness. Furthermore, dandelion root has been traditionally used to treat issues related to digestive health, such as bloating and indigestion, which can also contribute to an unpleasant bitter taste in the mouth.
remedy_constituents: Flavonoids: These compounds have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce inflammation in the mouth and alleviate bitter taste.
Triterpenoid saponins: These saponins can help reduce bacterial growth and inflammation in the mouth, contributing to the relief of bitter taste.
Phenolic acids: These acids have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce oxidative stress and inflammation in the mouth, leading to relief from bitter taste.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Leaves are used to make teas for their bitter taste due to their high concentration of sesquiterpene lactones, which give them a bitter flavor.
Roots: Roots are used to make teas for their bitter taste, as they contain a higher concentration of taraxasterol, a compound responsible for their bitter flavor.
Stems: Stems are also used to make teas for their bitter taste, as they contain a mixture of sesquiterpene lactones and other compounds that contribute to their bitter flavor.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 20-30 fresh taraxacum officinale leaves and flowers in the early morning when dew is present.
Rinse the leaves and flowers under cool running water to remove any dirt or debris.
Chop the leaves and flowers finely to increase their surface area for infusion.
Steep 1-2 teaspoons of chopped taraxacum officinale in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove solids and serve.

remedies.remedy_desc: Achillea millefolium teas helps with bitter taste in mouth because of its natural anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties. The plant's active compounds, such as sesquiterpenes and flavonoids, work to combat the bacteria and inflammation that can cause a bitter taste. The tea also contains antioxidants that help protect the mouth from damage and promote a healthy balance of oral flora, reducing the likelihood of a bitter taste. This makes it a popular remedy for individuals experiencing a bitter taste in the mouth.
remedy_constituents: Chlorogenic Acid: This phenolic compound helps to reduce bitterness by inhibiting the activity of enzymes that contribute to the perception of bitterness, thereby providing a soothing effect on the mouth.

Sesquiterpene Lactones: These compounds, including achillin and leucodin, have anti-inflammatory properties that may help to alleviate bitter taste by reducing inflammation and irritation in the oral cavity.

Flavonoids (including Quercetin): As antioxidants, flavonoids like quercetin help to neutralize free radicals and reduce oxidative stress in the mouth, which may contribute to a decrease in bitter taste.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of Achillea millefolium are used to make teas with a bitter taste in the mouth due to their high content of sesquiterpene lactones, which have bitter and astringent properties.

Roots: The roots of Achillea millefolium are used to make teas with a bitter taste in the mouth due to their high concentration of sesquiterpene lactones and phenolic compounds, which contribute to their bitter and astringent flavor.

Stems: The stems of Achillea millefolium are used to make teas with a bitter taste in the mouth due to their content of sesquiterpene lactones and other phenolic compounds, which are responsible for their bitter and astringent taste.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh leaves and flowers of the plant by cutting them at the base with scissors at dawn.
Dry the harvested plant material in a warm place with air circulation for 7 to 10 days.
Combine 1 teaspoon of dried plant material with 8 ounces of boiling water and let steep for 5 minutes.
Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth and discard the solids.
Serve the tea hot or cold and consume immediately to enjoy its benefits.

remedies.remedy_desc: Andrographis paniculata teas helps with bitter taste in mouth because it contains compounds that help reduce the presence of volatile sulfur compounds (VSCs) in the mouth. These VSCs are responsible for causing bad breath and a bitter taste. The tea's antioxidants, including andrographolide, may also help neutralize the VSCs, thereby alleviating the symptoms. Additionally, the anti-inflammatory properties of Andrographis paniculata may help soothe and balance the oral microbiome, leading to a fresher, cleaner mouth.
remedy_constituents: Andrographolide: This constituent has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce swelling and irritation in the mouth, thereby alleviating bitter taste.
Neoadrographolide: It has been found to have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, which can help protect the mouth from damage and reduce the perception of bitter taste.
Lignans: These polyphenolic compounds may help mitigate the bitter taste by reducing the formation of free radicals and inflammation in the mouth, thus promoting overall oral health.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Leaves are used to make teas for bitter taste in mouth, as they are rich in andrographolide, a compound responsible for the bitter taste and medicinal properties of Andrographis paniculata.
Roots: Roots are used to make teas for bitter taste in mouth, as they contain a high concentration of andrographolide and other bitter compounds.
Rhyzomes: Rhyzomes are used to make teas for bitter taste in mouth, as they are a key source of andrographolide and other bitter compounds with medicinal properties.
remedy_recipe: Gather 30-60 grams of dried andrographis paniculata root and leaves from a reputable source.
Rinse the dried herbs under cold running water to remove any impurities.
Heat one liter of water in a saucepan until it reaches a rolling boil for five minutes.
Steep the andrographis paniculata in the boiling water for 10-15 minutes or until the liquid turns dark green.
Strain the tea through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a cup to remove the solids.

remedies.remedy_desc: Cynara scolymus teas helps with bitter taste in mouth because it contains inulin, a type of soluble fiber that can help reduce the production of volatile sulfur compounds in the mouth. These compounds are responsible for the characteristic bitter taste of bad breath. By binding to these compounds, inulin can help neutralize their effects and leave the mouth feeling fresher and cleaner. Additionally, Cynara scolymus teas have anti-inflammatory properties that can help soothe and protect the gums and oral tissues.
remedy_constituents: Saponins: Help to reduce bitter taste in mouth by inhibiting the production of bitter compounds and enhancing the sense of sweetness.
Cynarin: Help to alleviate bitter taste in mouth by blocking the receptors on the tongue that perceive bitterness and also by enhancing the sense of sweetness.
Inulin: Help to reduce bitter taste in mouth by forming a protective barrier on the tongue that prevents the perception of bitter compounds.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Cynara scolymus leaves are used to make teas for their bitter taste due to their high concentration of sesquiterpene lactones and flavonoids.

Flowers: Cynara scolymus flowers are used to make teas for their bitter taste due to their high content of sesquiterpene lactones and flavonoids, which provide a distinctive flavor and health benefits.

Buds: Cynara scolymus buds are used to make teas for their bitter taste due to their high concentration of sesquiterpene lactones and flavonoids, which are similar in composition to the leaves and flowers.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 20-30 fresh cynara scolymus flowers in the early morning, preferably when the dew is still present on the plant.
Rinse the cynara scolymus flowers gently with filtered water to remove any dirt or debris, 2 minutes preparation time.
Combine the rinsed cynara scolymus flowers with 1 liter of boiling water in a large heat-resistant glass container, 5 minutes preparation time.
Allow the mixture to steep for 5-7 minutes, then strain the liquid through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve.
Store the herbal tea in the refrigerator and consume within 24 hours, 1 tablespoon or 15ml dosage recommended.

remedies.remedy_desc: Cinchona officinalis teas helps with bitter taste in mouth because of its unique properties. The alkaloids present in the plant, such as quinine and cinchonine, have been traditionally used to treat various health issues, including mouth infections and gum inflammation. These compounds have antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory effects, which help to reduce the bacteria that cause bad breath and a bitter taste. By soothing and cleansing the mouth, Cinchona officinalis teas promote a healthy oral environment, leading to fresher breath and a more pleasant taste experience.
remedy_constituents: Quinovic acids: These compounds have astringent properties, which help to reduce the sensation of bitterness in the mouth by drying out the mucous membranes and reducing inflammation.

Quinoline alkaloids (including quinine and cinchonine): These compounds have a bitter taste, but they also have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which help to soothe and heal the mucous membranes in the mouth, reducing the perception of bitterness.

Cinchonine: This alkaloid has a bitter taste, but it also has anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which help to reduce inflammation and infection in the mouth, thereby reducing the sensation of bitterness.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves are the primary source of quinine, a bitter compound responsible for the distinctive taste of Cinchona tea.
Barks: The bark of Cinchona officinalis contains cinchonine and quinidine, which are bitter compounds that contribute to the tea's flavor.
Fruits: The fruits of Cinchona officinalis contain bitter compounds such as cinchonic acid, which adds to the tea's bitter taste.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 20-30 grams of fresh leaves and stems from the cinchona officinalis plant in the morning.
Dry the harvested material in a low-temperature oven at 30 degrees celsius for 2 hours.
Grind 1-2 teaspoons of dried cinchona officinalis into a fine powder using a mortar and pestle.
Steep 1/2 teaspoon of the powdered cinchona officinalis in 250 milliliters of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea and let it cool to room temperature for 10-15 minutes before consumption.

remedies.remedy_desc: Centaurium erythraea teas helps with bitter taste in mouth because it has been traditionally used to address oral health issues. The plant's anti-inflammatory properties may soothe irritated tissues in the mouth, reducing the sensation of bitterness. Additionally, its antimicrobial properties may help combat the growth of bacteria that can cause bad breath and an unpleasant taste. By promoting oral health, Centaurium erythraea teas may alleviate bitter taste in the mouth, providing a refreshing and revitalizing experience. This has led to its use as a natural remedy for various oral health concerns.
remedy_constituents: Iridoids: Iridoids help with bitter taste in mouth by neutralizing or masking the bitter compounds in the saliva and tongue.
Phenolic acids: Phenolic acids help with bitter taste in mouth by reducing the activity of bitter receptors on the tongue and in the saliva.
Flavonoids: Flavonoids help with bitter taste in mouth by inhibiting the production or release of bitter chemicals in the mouth.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Leaves are used to make teas for their bitter taste due to the presence of compounds like sesquiterpene glycosides.
Roots: Roots are used for their bitter flavor and medicinal properties, which include compounds like iridoid glycosides.
Stems: Stems are also used to make teas with a bitter taste, containing compounds like sesquiterpene glycosides that contribute to their flavor.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 20-30 fresh or dried roots of centaurium erythraea in late summer or early fall season.
Clean the harvested roots by gently rinsing them with filtered water to remove any dirt particles.
Dry the roots in a warm place for 7-10 days or until they become brittle and lightweight.
Steep 1-2 teaspoons of dried centaurium erythraea roots in 1 cup of boiling water for 5-10 minutes.
Strain the tea and discard the solids by pouring it through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup.

remedies.remedy_desc: Artemisia vulgaris teas helps with bitter taste in mouth because it contains compounds that reduce bacteria growth, leading to a decrease in volatile sulfur compounds (VSCs) responsible for the bitter taste. The tea's antimicrobial properties target the bacteria in the mouth, such as Streptococcus and Lactobacillus, which contribute to VSCs production. By controlling these bacteria, the tea helps alleviate bitter taste, promoting a fresher and cleaner mouth environment. This makes it a potential remedy for halitosis and other oral health issues.
remedy_constituents: Phenolic acids: They help reduce bitterness in the mouth by neutralizing free radicals and promoting oral health, which in turn alleviates bitter taste.
Flavonoids: These compounds help reduce bitterness by inhibiting the activity of enzymes involved in bitterness perception, leading to a decrease in bitter taste.

Terpenoids (including sesquiterpenes): They help alleviate bitter taste in the mouth by modulating the expression of genes involved in bitter taste perception and reducing inflammation in the oral cavity.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Their bitter compounds help stimulate digestion and relieve digestive issues.
Stems: The bitter properties in the stems help to stimulate digestion and can aid in appetite suppression.
Roots: The roots contain a high concentration of bitter compounds that can aid in digestive issues and provide a sense of relief.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 1-2 ounces of fresh or dried leaves and flowers from the plant in the morning.
Rinse the harvested plant material with cold water to remove any dirt or debris.
Chop the plant material into smaller pieces to increase the surface area for infusion.
Steep 1 teaspoon of the chopped plant material in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea and discard the solids to enjoy the herbal tea.
